perturbation.original_text: "Subsidiary" shall mean: (i) a corporation, company or other entity, in which a ---------- Party now or hereafter, owns or controls, directly or indirectly, fifty percent (50%) or more of the outstanding shares or securities (representing the right to vote for the election of directors or other managing authority), provided, however, that such corporation, company, or other entity shall be deemed to be a Subsidiary only so long as such ownership or control exists; or (ii) an entity which does not have outstanding shares or securities, as may be the case in a partnership, joint venture or unincorporated association, but in which a Party now or hereafter, owns or controls, directly or indirectly, fifty percent (50%) or more of the ownership interest representing the right to make the decisions for such entity, provided, however, that such entity shall be deemed to be a Subsidiary only so long as such ownership or control exists.
perturbation.changed_text: "Subsidiary" shall mean: (i) a corporation, company or other entity, in which a ---------- Party now or hereafter, owns or controls, directly or indirectly, twenty-five percent (25%) or more of the outstanding shares or securities (representing the right to vote for the election of directors or other managing authority), provided, however, that such corporation, company, or other entity shall be deemed to be a Subsidiary only so long as such ownership or control exists; or (ii) an entity which does not have outstanding shares or securities, as may be the case in a partnership, joint venture or unincorporated association, but in which a Party now or hereafter, owns or controls, directly or indirectly, twenty-five percent (25%) or more of the ownership interest representing the right to make the decisions for such entity, provided, however, that such entity shall be deemed to be a Subsidiary only so long as such ownership or control exists.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Subsidiary' is changed from 50% ownership to 25% ownership. This creates a conflict because commonly understood legal definitions and accounting standards use a higher threshold (typically over 50%) for determining control and therefore subsidiary status. This could impact how the contract is interpreted with regards to control and liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the SEC's definition of 'control' which is used to determine the status of a subsidiary. The SEC generally considers control to exist when an entity has the power to direct the management and policies of another entity, usually indicated by ownership of more than 50% of the voting securities. Changing the percentage to 25% creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance with securities regulations.

(13) The terms "buy" and "purchase" each include any contract to buy, purchase, or otherwise acquire. For security futures products, such term includes any contract, agreement, or transaction for future delivery. For security-based swaps, such terms include the execution, termination (prior to its scheduled maturity date), assignment, exchange, or similar transfer or conveyance of, or extinguishing of rights or obligations under, a security-based swap, as the context may require. (14) The terms "sale" and "sell" each include any contract to sell or otherwise dispose of. For security futures products, such term includes any contract, agreement, or transaction for future delivery. For security-based swaps, such terms include the execution, termination (prior to its scheduled maturity date), assignment, exchange, or similar transfer or conveyance of, or extinguishing of rights or obligations under, a security-based swap, as the context may require. (15) The term "Commission" means the Securities and Exchange Commission established by section 78d of this title . (16) The term "State" means any State of the United States, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, or any other possession of the United States. (17) The term "interstate commerce" means trade, commerce, transportation, or communication among the several States, or between any foreign country and any State, or between any State and any place or ship outside thereof. The term also includes intrastate use of (A) any facility of a national securities exchange or of a telephone or other interstate means of communication, or (B) any other interstate instrumentality. (18) The term "person associated with a broker or dealer" or "associated person of a broker or dealer" means any partner, officer, director, or branch manager of such broker or dealer (or any person occupying a similar status or performing similar functions), any person directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by, or under common control with such broker or dealer, or any employee of such broker or dealer, except that any person associated with a broker or dealer whose functions are solely clerical or ministerial shall not be included in the meaning of such term for purposes of section 78o(b) of this title (other than paragraph (6) thereof). (19) The terms "investment company", "affiliated person", "insurance company", "separate account", and "company" have the same meanings as in the Investment Company Act of 1940 [ 15 U.S.C. 80a–1 et seq. ]. (20) The terms "investment adviser" and "underwriter" have the same meanings as in the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 [ 15 U.S.C. 80b–1 et seq. ]. (21) The term "person associated with a member" or "associated person of a member" when used with respect to a member of a national securities exchange or registered securities association means any partner, officer, director, or branch manager of such member (or any person occupying a similar status or performing similar functions), any person directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by, or under common control with such member, or any employee of such member. (22)(A) The term "securities information processor" means any person engaged in the business of (i
perturbation.justification: The modified text lowers the ownership percentage required to define a subsidiary from 50% to 25%. This contradicts the common understanding and legal definition of 'control' as typically involving ownership of more than 50% of voting securities, as indicated in the Law Explanation. The SEC generally considers control to exist when an entity has the power to direct the management and policies of another entity, which is usually indicated by ownership of more than 50% of the voting securities. While the scraped snippets don't directly state a 50% threshold for subsidiary control, they do reference the SEC and related securities regulations, implying the existence of such standards, which the change undermines, creating a potential for non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Section 6.02. Nortel Benefit from Cross Licenses. Subject to the terms and ----------------------------------- conditions of this Agreement, Entrust, to the extent of its legal right to do so, hereby grants to Nortel, under the Entrust Patents, an irrevocable, non- transferable, non-assignable, indivisible, non-exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide license for Licensed Products and Licensed Services.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 6.02. Nortel Benefit from Cross Licenses. Subject to the terms and ----------------------------------- conditions of this Agreement, Entrust, to the extent of its legal right to do so, hereby grants to Nortel, under the Entrust Patents, a revocable, non- transferable, non-assignable, indivisible, non-exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide license for Licensed Products and Licensed Services.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'irrevocable' to 'revocable' significantly alters the nature of the license. An irrevocable license is generally considered a permanent right, whereas a revocable license can be terminated by the grantor. This creates uncertainty about the stability of the cross-licensing agreement, and its alignment with intellectual property laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian law (where the agreement is governed) recognizes 'irrevocable' licenses as creating a more secure right, a change to 'revocable' reduces the security of that right.
perturbation.law_citation: Canadian Patent Act, RSC 1985, c P-4
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the license from irrevocable to revocable, we are creating a scenario which does not follow the Canadian law of patents. While Canadian law allows for revocable licenses, such a dramatic change would make the entire licensing agreement questionable and unstable because the licensee may not make proper investments.

perturbation.justification: The change from "irrevocable" to "revocable" introduces a significant legal contradiction related to intellectual property licensing. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that an irrevocable license is a permanent right, whereas a revocable license can be terminated. The Law Explanation suggests that such a change could destabilize the agreement and discourage investment. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, related to the Patent Act, does not explicitly define "irrevocable" or "revocable" licenses, the change directly impacts the nature of the agreement and its enforceability, fulfilling the conditions for a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section 12.06. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and be --------- ---- construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario, Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 12.06. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and be --------- ---- construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Quebec, Canada.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the governing law from Ontario to Quebec. These two provinces have different legal systems (Ontario uses common law, Quebec uses civil law for many private matters). This creates conflict because the contract's language and interpretation will now be subject to a completely different legal framework. It can cause problems in court.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Canadian Constitution recognizes the distinct legal systems of the provinces. Using a different jurisdiction causes a contradiction because the intent of the document, as drafted with Ontario law in mind, would be interpreted differently under Quebec civil law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By modifying the governing law from Ontario to Quebec, the agreement now comes under civil law, in some cases, compared to common law. This introduces a source of instability as the interpretations of legal clauses change as a result.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Ontario law, while the changed contract specifies Quebec law. The perturbation explanation specifies that Ontario uses common law and Quebec uses civil law, which creates a conflict because contract interpretation is now subject to a completely different legal framework. The law explanation supports the perturbation explanation by stating that interpreting legal clauses may change as a result of the legal system changing. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly discuss common or civil law, the change in governing law, coupled with the explanation of differing legal systems, indicates a legal contradiction, because the contract's interpretation and enforcement will be subject to a fundamentally different legal framework, making it potentially unenforceable under the originally intended legal system (Ontario).
